title: Jesus our groom part 1
scripture_text: “Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God believe also in me. My Father’s house has many rooms; if that were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you? And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am. You know the way to the place where I am going.” Thomas said to him, “Lord, we don’t know where you are going, so how can we know the way?” Jesus answered, ‘I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. If you really know me, you will know my Father as well. From now on, you do know him and have seen him. Philip said, “Lord, show us the Father and that will be enough for us.” Jesus answered: “Don’t you know me, Philip, even after I have been among you such a long time? Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, ‘Show us the Father’? Don’t you believe that I am in the Father, and that the Father is in me? The words I say to you I do not speak on my own authority. Rather, it is the Father, living in me, who is doing his work. Believe me when I say that I am in the Father and the Father is in me; or at least believe on the evidence of the works themselves. Very truly I tell you, whoever believes in me will do the works I have been doing, and they will do even greater things than these, because I am going to the Father. And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son. You may ask me for anything in my name, and I will do it.’’
devotional:
The passage we are looking at is a very well known passage, it is a passage that is often used as an evangelistic or an apologetic text that shows people that Jesus is the only way to the Father.

Although it may be legitimate to use the text in the way, I don’t think that this was its intended purpose. Jesus is speaking to those who have made a commitment to follow him. He is speaking to people who’s world is about to fall apart. This is a pastoral passage, were Jesus wants to equip those who love him with the resources to cope with the inevitability of trouble, uncertainty, confusion and fear.

Part of the problem with this passage, in my view, is that it has been ‘over heaven-ised.’ By that I mean that most people assume that Jesus is referring to heaven in this passage and his purpose is to generate hope in the hearts of his disciples. I am sure there is an element of truth in this kind of application, but I don’t think this was Jesus’ intention. Nor do I think the disciples would have thought about heaven when they heard these words.

I think that the over emphasis to heaven has robbed these verses of some of their present day application. In John 16:1 Jesus said, ‘All this I have told you so that you will not fall away.’ The idea behind the word for fall away is that of an animal caught in a snare or a trap, with the complete loss of freedom.

The words that Jesus spoke at this time were to help his disciples to remain faithful, to keep going. You could say than in John chapters 14-16 Jesus defines faithfulness.

So how are we to understand John 14:1-14?

Before we look at our passage let me give you some cultural background.

We know that what Jesus says here is in the setting of a passover meal. The passover meal had two main functions, one was to remember the great deliverance God accomplished when he rescued his people from Egypt. The second purpose of the passover was to remember the covenant God had made with his people Israel. The covenant God made at Sinai was seen in two distinct ways. It was seen as a king making at agreement with his subjects. But, it was also seen and understood as a marriage covenant.

In Exodus 6:6-7 we read these words: -

‘’Therefore, say to the Israelites: ‘I am the Lord, and I will bring you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians. I will free you from being slaves to them, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with mighty acts of judgment. I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God.’’

Here God promises to do 4 things: -

[1] I will bring you out, [2] I will free you, [3] I will redeem you, [4] I will take you as my own.

Over time these promises became the words that were used in a Jewish marriage proposal. This is what a young man would say to the lady he wanted to marry. This proposal was also re-enacted during the passover. During the passover they would drink from the cup four times, each time would represent one of the four promises. The passover was a proposal meal.
prayer: Father thank you that Jesus as our groom wants an intimate relationship with us.
html:
Jesus our groom part 1

Study Text: John 14:1-14
“Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God believe also in me. My Father’s house has many rooms; if that were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you? And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am. You know the way to the place where I am going.” Thomas said to him, “Lord, we don’t know where you are going, so how can we know the way?” Jesus answered, ‘I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. If you really know me, you will know my Father as well. From now on, you do know him and have seen him. Philip said, “Lord, show us the Father and that will be enough for us.” Jesus answered: “Don’t you know me, Philip, even after I have been among you such a long time? Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, ‘Show us the Father’? Don’t you believe that I am in the Father, and that the Father is in me? The words I say to you I do not speak on my own authority. Rather, it is the Father, living in me, who is doing his work. Believe me when I say that I am in the Father and the Father is in me; or at least believe on the evidence of the works themselves. And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son. You may ask me for anything in my name, and I will do it.’’

The passage we are looking at is a very well known passage, it is a passage that is often used as an evangelistic or an apologetic text that shows people that Jesus is the only way to the Father.

Although it may be legitimate to use the text in the way, I don’t think that this was its intended purpose. Jesus is speaking to those who have made a commitment to follow him. This is a pastoral passage, were Jesus wants to equip those who love him with the resources to cope with the inevitability of trouble, uncertainty, confusion and fear.

Part of the problem with this passage, in my view, is that it has been ‘over heaven-ised.’ By that I mean that most people assume that Jesus is referring to heaven in this passage and his purpose is to generate hope in the hearts of his disciples. Before we look at our passage let me give you some cultural background.

The second purpose of the passover was to remember the covenant God had made with his people Israel. The covenant God made at Sinai was seen in two distinct ways. It was seen as a king making at agreement with his subjects. But, it was also seen and understood as a marriage covenant.

In Exodus 6:6-7 we read these words: -

Here God promises to do 4 things: -

[1] I will bring you out, [2] I will free you, [3] I will redeem you, [4] I will take you as my own.

Over time these promises became the words that were used in a Jewish marriage proposal. This is what a young man would say to the lady he wanted to marry. This proposal was also re-enacted during the passover. During the passover they would drink from the cup four times, each time would represent one of the four promises. The passover was a proposal meal.

Prayer: Father thank you that Jesus as our groom wants an intimate relationship with us.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Psalms 23-26
